text: Good morning today we will learn how to use the da Vinci robot for continuous suturing first you have to make a dandy jamming knot outside and that is actually a loop within loop and then you take the bite and after taking the needle out the needle should be taken from within this dandy jamming knot so that without making a C and reverse C you will have a starter knot and you can start with the locking the knot here we can see just by pulling the suture the loop will start becoming smaller and then you can introduce inside and then you can take the suture out care should be taken that the needle end of the suture has to be taken out

The surgeon demonstrates continuous suturing using the da Vinci robotic system. The needle end of the suture is pulled to lock the stitch, utilizing an endoskeletal and half-circle needle. The surgeon forms a 'C' shape with the suture to take bites, ensuring each stitch locks automatically during the continuous suturing process.

you don't have to lock it and due to the wrist articulation you can pull it very nicely tight it and again take the another suture another bite and that way continuous suturing can be used for the serous surface of the myoma as well as we are using it for the gastric placation and sometime in hernia surgery for the peritoneal closure in da Vinci continuous suturing has particularly role in the cases of the radical prostatectomy and sometimes the partial nephrectomy for the closure of the capsule in those situation it will be faster and you will not have to use the multiple suture with one twenty four centimeter suture

With the continuous suturing you can close approximately fifteen centimeter intratummy line So again this is continuing and you will hold the needle Generally we should try to hold the needle at the junction of two-third and one-third just like the principle of the open surgery and in da Vinci to align the needle you can pass it from one hand to another

The surgeon demonstrates the termination of a suture using the Aberdeen technique. A loop within a loop is created, and the non-needle end is pulled to lock the suture. The surgeon ensures the suture is tightened and carefully handles the suture to avoid trapping the needle.
The surgeon demonstrates the termination of the Aberdeen knot in da Vinci robotic suturing by pulling the suture near the needle end, effectively locking the knot.
